gotchas.note: Health departments require a plan review of kitchen layout before buildout begins; if equipment placement (e.g., handwashing sink distance from prep surface) does not match approved drawings, inspectors will require physical changes before issuing a pass, triggering 2-6 week re-inspection delays.

source.title: FDA Food Code - FDA

gotchas.note: In most states a liquor license application requires 3-6 months and a single neighbor protest or proximity violation (within 200 ft of a school or house of worship, or 500-ft saturation rule) can trigger a formal hearing adding 2-6 additional months; this is the single longest-lead item for any full-service restaurant.

source.title: Michigan Liquor Control Code MCL 436.1503 - Proximity to Church or School

gotchas.note: Hood suppression system specs (nozzle placement, agent type, BTU coverage) must exactly match the installed equipment make and model; a mismatch or missing service certification causes fire inspection failure and a 2-4 week re-inspection wait.

source.title: NFPA 96: Standard for Ventilation Control and Fire Protection of Commercial Cooking Operations

gotchas.note: Signing a lease before confirming zoning is the most commonly cited operator mistake; if the site requires a conditional use permit or zoning variance, public hearings add 3-6 months and the permit may be denied entirely, forfeiting any non-refundable lease deposits already paid.

source.title: Restaurant Permits, Zoning, and Compliance: What You Need Before Opening

gotchas.note: A commissary kitchen agreement with a licensed commercial facility must be on file with the health department before the mobile food unit permit inspection is scheduled; missing or non-compliant commissary documentation is the most common reason health permit applications are denied for food trucks (source: PermitDue food truck permit guide, 2026; Houston Food Truck Association 2026 guide).

source.title: Mobile Food Service Permits - Salt Lake County Health Department (commissary requirement)

gotchas.note: In major metros (NYC, LA), mobile food vending unit permits are issued through a lottery or waitlist system with a fixed quota; operators can be fully licensed yet wait 6-18 months for an available permit slot before they can legally operate on public streets.

source.title: NYC Health Department Mobile Food Vending Waiting List - NYC.gov

gotchas.note: A food truck operating in multiple cities typically needs a separate business license (and sometimes a separate health permit) in each jurisdiction; operators who discover this only after their first cross-city event face immediate shutdown and fines starting at $250-$2,000 per violation.

source.title: Food Cart Vendor Permit - NYC311 (multi-jurisdiction permit requirements)

gotchas.note: Every piece of installed equipment must exactly match the plans submitted during plan review; substitutions made during truck build-out (e.g., swapping a fryer brand) require re-submission and re-inspection, adding 2-4 weeks and a resubmission fee.

source.title: Commercial Commissary Kitchen Permit - King County Public Health

references.title: NYC Mobile Food Vending Permits and Licenses - NYC311
Salt Lake County Mobile Food Service Permits
King County Commercial Commissary Kitchen Permit
Food Truck Commissary Requirements Guide

gotchas.note: The SLA 500-foot rule requires an additional public-interest hearing whenever three or more existing on-premises licenses are within 500 feet of the applicant; nearly every block in dense urban neighborhoods triggers this rule, adding 30-90 days and requiring applicants to affirmatively justify the community benefit of another licensed premises.

source.title: The 500 Foot Rule - New York State Liquor Licensing

gotchas.note: All principals with 10% or more ownership interest must disclose prior alcohol license revocations, criminal history, and undisclosed silent-partner interests; omissions discovered during background check cause automatic delay and can result in denial of the entire application.

source.title: What You Need to Know to Become a Licensed Retailer — New York State Liquor Authority

gotchas.note: Assembly-occupancy nightclubs require a fire-safety plan review at the state fire marshal level before local fire inspection; if the fire marshal identifies occupancy-load or egress deficiencies, structural changes to the buildout may be required before any other approvals can proceed, costing weeks and significant contractor fees.

source.title: Assembly Occupancies - NFPA-Based Guide to Life Safety, Egress, and Controls

gotchas.note: Entertainment licenses often require proof of music performance rights (ASCAP, BMI, or SESAC) and a noise/security plan; jurisdictions in dense residential areas routinely impose hour restrictions or decibel caps that conflict with planned operations, which are not discoverable until the license review stage.

source.title: Music Licensing for Bars, Restaurants, Breweries - BMI

references.title: NY SLA Liquor License Application Portal
NFPA 101 Life Safety Code - Assembly Occupancies
BMI Music Licensing for Bars and Restaurants
ASCAP Music Licensing FAQs

gotchas.note: FDA food facility registration requires a DUNS (Dun & Bradstreet) number before the registration portal will accept the submission; bakeries that skip this prerequisite discover it only when the online form rejects their application, adding 1-2 weeks to obtain the DUNS number before they can re-apply.

source.title: Registration of Food Facilities and Other Submissions — FDA

gotchas.note: Health departments conduct a plan review of kitchen layout before any construction begins; if three-compartment sink placement, handwashing station location, or ventilation hood specs do not comply with the FDA Food Code as adopted by the state, inspectors will require physical modifications before issuing the permit, typically causing 4-8 week delays.

source.title: FDA Food Code — FDA

gotchas.note: FDA facility registration must be renewed biennially in even-numbered years (Oct-Dec); a lapsed registration during the renewal window makes the facility legally non-compliant and bars interstate shipment of any product until renewal is completed, which can take 1-4 weeks.

source.title: FSMA Final Rule on Amendments to Registration of Food Facilities — FDA

gotchas.note: Bakeries producing goods for wholesale (not just retail walk-in) are often classified as 'food manufacturing' rather than 'retail food establishment' under local zoning codes; a retail-zoned space may not permit manufacturing use, requiring a conditional use permit or relocation before any other permits can proceed.

source.title: U.S. Bakery NAICS and SIC Codes: Manufacturing vs. Retail Classification and Zoning

references.title: FDA Food Facility Registration - FSMA Requirements
FSMA Final Rule on Amendments to Registration of Food Facilities
FDA Food Code
Bakery Manufacturing vs. Retail Zoning Classification Guide
DUNS Number Requirements for Food Facility Registration - Association of Food Industries

gotchas.note: Catering operations that do not operate a fixed kitchen must provide a signed commissary agreement with a licensed commercial kitchen before the health department will schedule the pre-operation inspection; finding a compliant commissary that has available capacity can take 4-8 weeks in high-demand markets, blocking the entire permitting timeline.

source.title: Catering and Home-Based Food Service Business Permits - King County Public Health

gotchas.note: Many states issue a separate 'catering license' in addition to the general food service permit; caterers who apply only for a food service establishment permit discover the additional catering-specific license requirement only when they attempt to serve food off-site, resulting in an immediate stop-work order at events.

source.title: Commissary Agreement for Temporary and Mobile Food Facilities - Sonoma County Environmental Health

gotchas.note: Caterers serving at temporary event venues must obtain a temporary food service permit from the local health department for each event in many jurisdictions; operating at a single unpermitted event can result in a $500-$5,000 fine and jeopardize the main catering license.

source.title: Food Vending at Temporary Events - NYC Health Department

gotchas.note: Caterers who cross county or city lines to serve events may need a business license in each operating jurisdiction; an operator licensed in their home city who caters a single corporate event in a neighboring county without that county's license risks shutdown and fines.

source.title: SBA - Apply for Licenses and Permits

gotchas.note: TTB Brewer's Notice applications are reviewed in strict receipt order; incomplete applications (missing Organizational Information forms for all principals with 10%+ interest, or facility diagrams that do not match actual construction) trigger a deficiency notice and restart the clock, with median processing currently 56-78 calendar days per TTB's own dashboard (March 2026).

source.title: Processing Times for Original Permit Applications — TTB

gotchas.note: Brewery wastewater (high-BOD rinse water, spent grain slurry, yeast) typically exceeds local sewer discharge limits and requires a pretreatment permit from the state environmental agency before connection to the municipal system; this permit involves engineering studies and can take 8-16 weeks, blocking the building permit sign-off and delaying opening.

source.title: Connecticut DEEP Brewery Permitting and Pollution Prevention Factsheet

gotchas.note: Most states require a separate state manufacturer's license (brewer's license) in addition to the TTB Brewer's Notice; the state license requires the federal TTB approval as a prerequisite, creating a sequential dependency that can extend the total alcohol-licensing timeline to 6-9 months when both agencies are at normal processing speeds.

source.title: Brewer's Notice — TTB: Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau

gotchas.note: Brewery buildouts often require reinforced floor drains rated for caustic CIP chemicals and floor load ratings for large fermentation vessels; if these are not specified in architectural drawings submitted to the building department, the plan review fails and drawings must be resubmitted, adding 4-8 weeks.

source.title: Wastewater — Brewers Association Engineers Resource

references.title: TTB Brewer's Notice - How to Qualify
TTB Processing Times for Original Permit Applications
EPA National Pretreatment Program - Local Limits
Connecticut DEEP Breweries Permitting and Pollution Prevention Factsheet
Brewers Association - Craft Brewery Startup Resources

gotchas.note: Coffee shops that serve any food items (pastries, sandwiches) are typically classified as a full food service establishment, not a limited food service operation; this triggers a higher-tier health permit with more extensive plan review requirements, including a dedicated handwashing sink at the bar that many build-outs omit until the inspector flags it.

source.title: FDA Food Code — Retail Food Protection

gotchas.note: Coffee shops with drive-through windows require a separate land use approval or conditional use permit in most jurisdictions even if the main café use is permitted by right; this is a common mid-design discovery that can add 3-6 months if a CUP public hearing is required.

source.title: CEQA Notice of Exemption - CUP No. 2024-064 New Drive-Thru Coffee Shop (Bell Gardens, CA)

gotchas.note: The manager-on-duty must hold an active certified food protection manager (CFPM) certificate (e.g., ServSafe) before the state health license will be issued; scheduling and passing the proctored exam can take 2-4 weeks, and the certificate must be in hand before the final health inspection.

source.title: Food Safety Manager Certification Requirements by State

gotchas.note: Illuminated, projecting, or window-graphic signs each require individual signage permits with dimension and illumination reviews; operators who install signage before permit approval face mandatory removal orders and must restart the permit process from scratch, adding 3-6 weeks.

source.title: SBA - Business Licenses and Permits (signage permits)

references.title: FDA Food Code
CFPM Requirements for Food Establishments - Minnesota Dept. of Health (example state portal)
Food Safety Manager Certification Requirements by State
Restaurant Permits, Zoning, and Compliance Guide

gotchas.note: TTB Bonded Winery permits require a detailed description of all winemaking equipment and premises; if the submitted diagram does not match the physical facility at the time of the TTB field investigation, the application is returned for correction and the median processing clock restarts (TTB reports median bonded winery processing of 62-77 calendar days as of early 2026).

source.title: Processing Times for Original Permit Applications (Bonded Winery) — TTB

gotchas.note: The USDA 'organic' certification process (NOP) is entirely separate from winery licensing and takes 3-6 months with an on-site inspection; wineries that plan to market any product as 'organic' or use USDA organic grapes must hold active organic certification before those label claims are legally valid, and TTB COLA (Certificate of Label Approval) approval cannot be obtained for organic claims without it.

source.title: Alcohol Beverages Labeled with Organic Claims — TTB

gotchas.note: State winery licenses are commonly restricted by production volume tier (e.g., 'farm winery,' 'small winery,' 'large winery'), and the tier determines which direct-to-consumer shipping and tasting room privileges apply; applying under the wrong tier requires a new application at the correct tier, adding 8-16 weeks.

source.title: California Winery Licensing: How to Open and Operate a Winery

gotchas.note: Every individual wine label sold commercially requires a separate TTB Certificate of Label Approval (COLA) before the product can legally be shipped or sold; wineries launching multiple SKUs discover that each vintage or blend variation is a new COLA application, and a government shutdown can freeze all COLA processing for weeks (as occurred during the October 2025 federal shutdown).

source.title: Certificate of Label Approval (COLA) — TTB

gotchas.note: Retail uses such as liquor stores, tobacco shops, or dense retail clusters may require a special use permit on top of standard zoning approval, adding 6-16 weeks; verifying allowed-use status before signing a lease is the single most common avoidable delay (source: Naperville Illinois Business Permit Directory 2026).

source.title: What Zoning is Required for Auto Repair? (Retail Special Use Permit Overview) - AutoLeap

gotchas.note: Tenant build-outs in existing commercial space still require a full permit review; applicants who submit plans without stamped architect/engineer drawings are routinely sent back for resubmission, triggering a fresh 4-12 week review cycle (source: Atlas Sign Industries permitting report).

source.title: Retail Tenant Improvement Permitting Guide | Commercial Projects - JDJ Consulting

gotchas.note: Applicants who enter a DBA or trade name that does not exactly match the Secretary of State filing trigger a manual identity review at the Department of Revenue, delaying permit issuance by 2-4 weeks (source: Avalara sales tax registration guide, 2022).

source.title: How to Avoid Sales Tax Registration Delays: Common Errors - online-tax-id-number.org

gotchas.note: Sign permits are jurisdiction-specific and often require a separate landlord authorization letter; illuminated or projecting signs in historic districts may need Board of Architectural Review approval adding another 4-8 weeks beyond the standard signage permit timeline (source: Atlas Sign Industries retail rollout report).

source.title: Historic District Board of Review - Illuminated Signs Checklist (Downtown Savannah) - MPC

gotchas.note: Cosmetology establishment licenses (distinct from individual practitioner licenses) require a premises inspection by the state licensing board; applicants who only obtain individual cosmetologist licenses and skip the separate salon establishment application are rejected at the business license stage — this is the #1 confusion reported by the Florida DBPR (source: floridacosmetologist.com).

source.title: Florida Salon License 2026: COSMO 6 Requirements - OpenExamPrep

gotchas.note: School transcripts submitted for hour verification must come directly from the cosmetology school to the state board; applicant-submitted photocopies are rejected as insufficient documentation, commonly adding 4-8 weeks to processing (source: smallbizhandbook.com Cosmetology & Salon License Guide 2026).

source.title: Request a Barbering and Cosmetology Letter of Certification or Transcript of Hours - Texas TDLR

gotchas.note: Health inspections for salons require shampoo bowls and sinks to drain to an approved sanitary sewer, not a floor drain; new buildouts that route drainage incorrectly fail inspection and require plumbing correction before re-inspection can be scheduled (source: Florida Department of Health cosmetology inspection checklist).

source.title: Cosmetology Inspection Program - MyFloridaLicense.com (Florida DBPR)

gotchas.note: Many cities require that all individual cosmetologist licenses held by employees be on file and posted at the salon before the business license is issued; hiring unlicensed staff or failing to post licenses causes the business license to be withheld or later suspended (source: MyFloridaLicense.com Cosmetology FAQs).

source.title: Florida DBPR Cosmetology Brochure - Steps to Opening a New Cosmetology Salon

references.title: Florida Salon License 2026: COSMO 6 Requirements - OpenExamPrep

gotchas.note: Barber shop establishment licenses are issued by the State Board of Barbers (separate from the cosmetology board) and require a separate shop application distinct from each individual barber's license; applicants who assume one license covers both are routinely denied at the business license stage (source: Florida DBPR barbershop FAQs; Virginia Board for Barbers and Cosmetology 18VAC41-50).

source.title: How to Open a Barbershop in California: Complete Guide 2026 - BarberíaClub

gotchas.note: Barbers licensed in another state must apply for reciprocity, which requires certified transcripts from the original barber school sent directly to the new state board; many applicants submit photocopies which are invalid and trigger resubmission cycles of 4-8 weeks (source: PA Department of State Cosmetology Licensure Guide).

source.title: Request a Barbering and Cosmetology Letter of Certification or Transcript of Hours - Texas TDLR

gotchas.note: Barber shops are inspected for the requirement that each barber chair has a dedicated, accessible handwashing sink within a specified distance (often 10 feet); landlord buildouts that locate sinks incorrectly fail inspection and require plumbing relocation before a re-inspection can be scheduled (source: Florida Department of Health barbershop inspection criteria).

source.title: North Carolina Board of Barber Examiners - New Barber Shop Inspection Checklist

gotchas.note: Barber shops in mixed-use or residential-adjacent zones sometimes require a conditional use permit or variance, adding 6-12 weeks beyond standard zoning clearance; this is triggered by classification as a 'personal services' use in zoning codes that separate it from general retail (source: Naperville Illinois business permit directory 2026).

gotchas.note: Most states require tattoo artists to complete a bloodborne pathogen (BBP) training course from an approved provider and submit the certificate with the license application; certificates from non-approved online providers are rejected, which is a leading cause of artist license application denial (source: Network for Public Health Law, Tattoo Regulation and Public Health 2024; LicenseMap.com).

source.title: Tattoo Licensing Requirements by State (2026) - TattooBizGuide

gotchas.note: The state Department of Health tattoo establishment permit requires documented proof that an autoclave (steam sterilizer) is on premises and has passed a spore test within the prior 30 days; shops that order equipment late discover this requirement only at inspection and face 4-8 week delays while equipment is procured and tested (source: tattoostudiopro.com health inspection checklist; Florida DOH tattoo establishment requirements).

source.title: Tattoo Establishment Licensure Requirements - Florida Department of Health

gotchas.note: County health inspections for tattoo parlors require every work station to have a non-porous surface (sealed concrete or tile — no carpet or fabric), a sink with hands-free faucet within the tattooing area, and sharps containers mounted at each station; stations that fail any of these three criteria receive a failed inspection and must schedule a full re-inspection (source: tattoostudiopro.com; Florida DOH).

source.title: Tattoo Establishment Licensure Requirements - Florida Department of Health

gotchas.note: Virginia and several other states updated tattooing regulations effective January 2026, reducing minimum training hour requirements but adding a new Hepatitis B vaccination documentation requirement for each artist on staff; shops that miss the vaccination record requirement are denied establishment renewal (source: Virginia Register of Regulations Vol. 42 Iss. 8, effective January 5, 2026).

gotchas.note: Dry cleaners using perchloroethylene (PERC) became subject to a 2024 EPA final rule under TSCA designating PERC as a carcinogen, with phased compliance and enhanced recordkeeping requirements under NESHAP Subpart M; new operators must determine which equipment tier applies and register under the correct Subpart M classification before starting operations or face retroactive violations (source: EPA Final Rule EPA-HQ-OAR-2005-0155, signed December 2024; EMAP Pennsylvania compliance guide March 2025).

source.title: FACT SHEET: 2024 Final Risk Management Rule for Perchloroethylene under TSCA - US EPA

gotchas.note: State environmental permits for PERC dry cleaners require submission of a leak detection plan, a perc consumption log format, and equipment specifications; applications missing any one of these three components are returned incomplete, and turnaround times of 8-16 weeks restart from zero on resubmission (source: Illinois Dry Cleaner Compliance Workbook 2025; Georgia Drycleaner Compliance Calendar).

source.title: Federal Register - NESHAP PCE Dry Cleaning Facilities Technology Review Final Rule (EPA-HQ-OAR-2005-0155)

gotchas.note: Many municipalities have enacted prohibitive zoning restrictions specifically against new PERC-based dry cleaners within a specified distance (often 300-1000 feet) of residential zones, daycare centers, or schools; operators who sign leases before zoning verification discover that perc use is outright prohibited at the chosen address (source: American Drycleaner regulatory update article).

source.title: Federal, State, and Local Policies Addressing Chemical Emissions from Dry Cleaners (ELI Report)

gotchas.note: Dry cleaners are required to maintain a monthly perc purchase and usage log beginning on the first day of operations under NESHAP Subpart M; failing to start this log on day one — even before first customer — constitutes a recordkeeping violation subject to civil penalties (source: Illinois SBEAP Dry Cleaner Compliance Workbook 2025).

gotchas.note: The ATF FFL application for a pawnbroker (Type 06 license) requires submitting fingerprint cards and passport photos by mail to ATF along with proof that the business premises address is the actual licensed location; applicants who list a home address or temporary address are denied because ATF conducts a premises inspection at the filed address before approval, and the ATF processing window is 8-12 weeks (source: ATF Publication 5300.15; 27 CFR § 771.43).

source.title: Apply for a Federal Firearms License (FFL) - Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives

gotchas.note: Pawn shops are classified as a 'conditional use' or 'special exception' in most commercial zoning codes due to secondhand goods regulations and proximity concerns; the Special Use Permit hearing before the Zoning Board of Appeals requires public notice posted 10-30 days in advance, and neighboring property owners can object, pushing the hearing to continuation cycles that add 6-16 additional weeks (source: ATF/zoning compliance guidance; Bravo Store Systems FFL compliance guide).

source.title: Utah Division of Consumer Protection - Pawnshops and Secondhand Dealers

gotchas.note: Pawnbrokers who deal in firearms must maintain an ATF-compliant Acquisition and Disposition (A&D) bound book from day one of operations; applications have been revoked post-approval for failure to have proper bound book infrastructure in place at the time of the ATF premises inspection that precedes license issuance (source: ATF FFL Quick Reference Guide, revised December 2021; Bravo Store Systems ATF compliance article February 2026).

source.title: ATF Bound Book Requirements - Firearms A&D Record - FFLGuard

gotchas.note: Many states require pawn shops to register with the state police or local law enforcement as a secondhand dealer in addition to the standard business license; this secondary registration is often overlooked and discovered only when operating, resulting in stop-work orders (source: Bravo Store Systems FFL compliance guides).

gotchas.note: Gyms are classified as assembly occupancy (Group A-3 or A-5 depending on jurisdiction) under the IBC, which carries stricter egress, sprinkler, and accessibility requirements than general commercial or retail use; a space zoned for retail or office that a gym operator assumes is suitable will fail the zoning/use-type check, requiring a conditional-use permit or variance that typically takes 8-12 weeks and may be denied by a planning commission (source: fmconsulting.net gym compliance guide; buildinginspectionauthority.com).

source.title: IBC 2021 Chapter 3 – Occupancy Classification and Use (ICC Digital Codes)

gotchas.note: Gyms with heavy free-weight areas, pool or sauna features, or large HVAC loads require structural engineering sign-offs and specialized mechanical permits in addition to the standard building permit; many gym owners discover these sub-permit requirements only during the plan-review stage, adding 3-6 weeks to approval timelines before construction can even begin (source: buildinginspectionauthority.com CO inspection requirements; fmconsulting.net).

source.title: IBC 2021 Chapter 3 – Occupancy Classification and Use (ICC Digital Codes)

gotchas.note: Many states tax gym memberships, personal training, and equipment rentals as taxable services; operators who register only for general sales tax but fail to identify the correct tax classification for fitness services may face retroactive sales tax assessments with penalties after opening (source: state Department of Revenue guidance; common audit finding across fitness industry).

gotchas.note: Counselors who work with minors or vulnerable adults face dual background check requirements: the professional licensing board background check AND a state childcare/elder care registry check; failure on either check blocks licensure regardless of the other passing.

source.title: Prospective Applicants with Criminal Backgrounds | Texas Behavioral Health Executive Council

gotchas.note: Many managed care panels (insurance networks) require LPCs/LMHCs to carry professional liability insurance as a condition of credentialing; the credentialing process takes 90-180 days and cannot begin until the license is active and insurance is bound, creating a compounded delay before the counselor can bill insurance.

source.title: Provider Credentialing Requirements | CAQH

gotchas.note: Home inspector licensing requirements vary dramatically: some states (e.g., Wyoming, South Dakota) have no state licensure requirement at all, while states like Texas require 194 hours of approved education, field experience under a licensed inspector, and passing a state exam; applicants who relocate from an unlicensed state must start the education process from zero.

source.title: Become a Real Estate Inspector | TREC

gotchas.note: The National Home Inspector Examination (NHIE), required by most licensing states, has a pass rate of approximately 70% for first-time takers and covers structural systems, roofing, plumbing, and electrical; candidates who use non-approved training providers find their course hours rejected by the state board, requiring re-enrollment in approved courses.

source.title: Texas Real Estate Inspector Examination Study Materials | TREC

gotchas.note: Home inspectors are required to carry Errors & Omissions (E&O) insurance in most licensing states; E&O premiums for home inspectors average $800-$2,500/year and many policies contain a per-inspection deductible ($1,000-$2,500) that new inspectors frequently overlook when pricing their services.

source.title: Home Inspector E&O Insurance Requirements | InspectorPro Insurance

gotchas.note: Home inspectors who offer ancillary services (mold testing, radon testing, sewer scoping) may need separate state-issued permits or certifications for each ancillary service, each with its own training, exam, and renewal requirements beyond the base home inspector license.

source.title: Mold Remediation in Schools and Commercial Buildings Guide | EPA

gotchas.note: The Appraisal Qualifications Board (AQB) mandates a trainee period under a certified supervisor before independent licensure; Certified Residential requires 1,500 hours over 12 months minimum, and Certified General requires 3,000 hours over 18 months minimum, making this one of the longest mandatory apprenticeship periods of any licensed profession.

source.title: Qualification Criteria | The Appraisal Foundation

gotchas.note: Finding a qualified supervisory appraiser is a documented bottleneck: many certified appraisers decline to supervise trainees due to liability exposure and low compensation for oversight time; some trainees wait 6-18 months before securing a supervisory relationship, delaying the start of their experience clock.

source.title: Criteria | The Appraisal Foundation

gotchas.note: The National Uniform Licensing and Certification Examinations (required for all levels above trainee) are administered through Pearson VUE and have a national pass rate of approximately 65-70%; Certified General has historically lower pass rates (~55%), and candidates who fail must wait 30 days between retakes.

source.title: National Uniform Licensing and Certification Examination | The Appraisal Foundation

gotchas.note: Appraiser Independence Requirements (AIR) under the Dodd-Frank Act prohibit lenders from directly selecting appraisers; appraisers who accept work directly from lenders without going through an Appraisal Management Company (AMC) may face compliance issues that result in assignments being rejected, affecting business viability immediately after licensing.

gotchas.note: The Professional Land Surveyor (PLS) exam follows a two-stage process: the Fundamentals of Surveying (FS) exam followed by the Professional Surveying (PS) exam, and most states require 4 years of progressive experience under a licensed PLS before the PS exam can be taken; NCEES reported a PS exam pass rate of approximately 55-65% for first-time takers.

source.title: Professional Surveying (PS) Exam | NCEES

gotchas.note: Several states (e.g., California, Texas) require a state-specific law and practice exam in addition to the NCEES PS exam; candidates who pass the national exam but fail the state supplement cannot be licensed until the state exam is retaken, adding 2-6 months.

source.title: Applying for Licensure as a Land Surveyor | California BPELSG

gotchas.note: Professional liability (E&O) insurance for surveyors averages $3,000-$8,000/year for a small firm and many policies exclude 'first-dollar defense' for boundary disputes, which are the most common surveyor claims; new surveyors frequently underestimate the cost and complexity of obtaining adequate coverage.

source.title: Licensure | NCEES

gotchas.note: Most states require surveying firms (not just individual surveyors) to obtain a Certificate of Authorization from the state licensing board; this corporate COA requires a designated responsible licensee (DRL) on file and must be updated within 30 days if the DRL changes, which is easy to miss during staff turnover.

gotchas.note: Incomplete plan sets — missing signed-and-sealed drawings, structural calculations, or energy compliance forms — are the single most common cause of immediate plan-check rejection, adding 2-6 weeks per revision cycle before the permit is issued. (Source: Neo-Scenic Designs, Ontario Building Permit guide, 2025)

source.title: Section 115 Stop Work Order — IBC 2021 (UpCodes)

(Source: EPA Demolition Information Packet, 2025)

gotchas.note: Commercial pesticide applicators must comply with EPA's 2017 Certification of Pesticide Applicators rule (40 CFR Part 171), which introduced new competency standards and exam requirements effective November 2024; businesses that started operations before the rule update and have employees certified under older standards are now required to complete additional training before their next recertification, or risk operating with uncertified applicators.

source.title: Federal Register: Pesticides — Certification of Pesticide Applicators (Final Rule, 40 CFR Part 171, Jan. 4, 2017)

gotchas.note: State pesticide applicator exams are offered on a fixed schedule (often quarterly) and require pre-registration; applicants who miss an exam date may wait 3–4 months for the next sitting, directly delaying the professional license and the business license that depends on it.

source.title: Wisconsin DATCP: 2026 Statewide Pesticide Applicator Certification Examination Schedule

gotchas.note: Several states require pesticide application businesses to file a pesticide-use reporting plan with the state environmental agency before commencing operations; this is distinct from and additional to the EPA certification and is commonly discovered only after the business license application is rejected.

source.title: How to Get Certified as a Pesticide Applicator — US EPA

gotchas.note: Restricted Use Pesticide (RUP) categories — soil fumigation, aerial application, and certain herbicide categories — each require a separate category certification; a business intending to offer multiple service types must pass multiple exams, significantly extending the licensing timeline.

source.title: 40 CFR § 171.103 — Standards for Certification of Commercial Applicators (LII/Cornell)

gotchas.note: Composting facilities in agricultural zones routinely trigger public hearings where neighbors raise odor, runoff, and fire-risk objections; planning commissions have denied permits on these grounds even for technically compliant applications, as documented in Barrow County (2025) and Stanislaus County (2025) proceedings.

source.title: Model Municipal Zoning Ordinance on Community Composting: With and Without Commentaries — NRDC

gotchas.note: Composting facilities that accept organic waste from third parties (e.g., food scraps from restaurants or biosolids) may trigger solid waste facility permitting under the Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA) in addition to state environmental permits, a classification that dramatically increases the regulatory burden and review timeline.

source.title: EPA Region 9: What Federal Regulations Exist for Composting? (RCRA Subtitle D)

gotchas.note: Leachate from outdoor compost piles discharging to the ground constitutes an unpermitted discharge under the Clean Water Act; operations that begin composting before state environmental permits are in place face administrative monetary penalties and mandatory remediation orders, as seen in multiple enforcement actions in 2025–2026.

source.title: EPA: Settlement — City of Columbia Landfill and Yard Waste Composting Facility Clean Water Act Violations

gotchas.note: Agricultural zoning does not automatically permit commercial-scale composting; many counties require a conditional use permit (CUP) or special use permit even on agriculturally-zoned land, and the distinction between farm-scale and commercial-scale composting varies widely by local ordinance.

source.title: California State Water Resources Control Board: Regulation of Composting Operations (General WDR)

gotchas.note: State cannabis cultivation licenses require applicants to submit proof of water supply source documentation at the time of application (e.g., a California Cannabis SIUR Registration Certificate); applicants who apply for the cannabis license before securing water rights documentation will receive an incomplete notice and must restart the application review clock.

source.title: California SWRCB: Cannabis Cultivation Water Rights — Cannabis SIUR

gotchas.note: California's Cannabis Cultivation Policy prohibits any surface water diversion during the dry-season forbearance period (April 1 through October 31); grow operations that do not pre-store sufficient wet-season water face mandatory curtailment orders during peak growing months, which can destroy an entire annual harvest.

source.title: California SWRCB: Cannabis Cultivation FAQs — Forbearance Period (April 1–October 31)

gotchas.note: Cannabis grow operations are prohibited in certain zones by local ordinance or federal policy overlap; many counties have adopted outright bans or strict setback requirements (e.g., 1,000 feet from schools or residences) that are discovered only after the state cannabis license application is underway, requiring site relocation.

source.title: Where Cannabis Businesses Are Allowed — California Department of Cannabis Control

gotchas.note: A cannabis grow operation in Florida was found in April 2026 to have violated its state Environmental Resource Permit (ERP) by improperly managing water discharge; ERP violations can result in permit suspension that halts all cultivation operations regardless of the cannabis license status.

source.title: Environmental Resource Permitting — Florida Department of Environmental Protection

gotchas.note: USDA NOP organic certification requires a minimum 36-month transition period during which no prohibited substances may be applied to the land; the clock starts from the last date any prohibited substance was applied — not from the application date — and any subsequent application of a prohibited substance (including pesticide drift from adjacent conventional fields) resets the full 36-month clock for the affected land parcel.

source.title: USDA AMS: Guide for Organic Crop Producers (36-Month Transition Period)

gotchas.note: Applications must be submitted to an NOP-accredited certifying agency at least 6 months before the target certification date; spring is the peak submission season and certifiers typically cannot expedite inspection scheduling, meaning late applicants routinely miss an entire growing season's window for certified organic sales.

source.title: USDA NOP: The Organic Certification Process (NOP 2601) — Inspection Scheduling and Peak Season Delays

gotchas.note: The Organic System Plan (OSP) must document every field, input, crop, and handling practice; omitting a single crop, storage space, or input — even inadvertently — gives the certifier grounds to issue a Notice of Noncompliance, which suspends the certification process until the OSP is corrected and re-inspected.

source.title: Oregon Tilth: Noncompliances and Adverse Actions in Organic Certification

gotchas.note: Organic certification from USDA does not replace state pesticide regulations; some states require organically-certified producers to maintain a separate state pesticide applicator certification even if they use only OMRI-listed substances, and the state Department of Agriculture permit review may surface this requirement after the USDA certification is already in hand.

source.title: How to Get Certified as a Pesticide Applicator — US EPA
